target.title: Surface Codex final answer candidates in activity
target.body:
## Summary

- Emit Codex app-server `phase: "final_answer"` answer-candidate activity records with `candidate`, `superseded`, and `selected` states.
- Route those `stream: "item"` / `kind: "answer_candidate"` records into the Control UI Activity model and Activity view, so dashboard users can see the candidate lifecycle instead of losing it.
- Keep answer-candidate activity out of IM/channel progress with `suppressChannelProgress: true`; final channel delivery still uses the selected final reply payload only.
- Localize the new Control UI Activity labels through the existing i18n locale pipeline.

## Real behavior proof

**Current-head proof update (June 13, 2026)**

Current PR head: `9c5e6f8acc56`.

This update addresses the latest ClawSweeper P1 finding that non-successful Codex attempts could appear in Control UI Activity as if a final answer candidate had been `selected`.

Code change now made at current head:
- `CodexAppServerEventProjector` tracks the terminal `turn/completed` status separately from the normalized turn snapshot.
- `answer_candidate` status is marked `selected` only when the authoritative Codex turn status is `completed`.
- Failed, aborted, timed-out, and client-closed attempts now terminate the active candidate as `superseded`, never `selected`.

Current-head regression proof:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts --testNamePattern="final-answer candidates|non-successful terminal attempts"
# PASS: 2 tests; selected is emitted for successful completed turns; failed/aborted/timed_out/no-turn-completion cases emit [candidate, superseded] and no selected.

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
# PASS: 81 tests

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts --testNamePattern="keeps suppressed answer candidates out of channel item progress"
# PASS: 1 focused test; answer-candidate activity stays suppressed from channel item progress.

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts --testNamePattern="answer candidates"
# PASS: 1 focused test; item events become Control UI activity entries.

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts --testNamePattern="answer candidate"
# PASS: 1 focused test; Control UI Activity renders the answer-candidate entry.
```

Inspectable event-path proof from the new regression:

```text
successful completed turn:
  answer_candidate msg-early-final    -> candidate -> superseded
  answer_candidate msg-selected-final -> candidate -> selected

non-success terminal attempts:
  failed turn                       -> candidate -> superseded
  aborted turn                      -> candidate -> superseded
  timed_out turn                    -> candidate -> superseded
  client close before turn/completed -> candidate -> superseded
  selected is never emitted for these non-success paths.
```

Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server can stream a `phase: "final_answer"` candidate, continue with tool work, then produce a later final answer. That native candidate history is real and useful for operator/debug surfaces, but messaging channels should still project one authoritative final answer. Before this update, answer-candidate `item` records were not connected to the Control UI Activity consumer; this PR now wires and renders them while keeping channel progress suppressed.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw repository on macOS, branch `agent/xiaozhua/codex-final-answer-candidates-rebase`, head `9c5e6f8acc56`, using the repository Vitest wrapper, the Control UI Activity renderer, the repository i18n checker, and a Vite-served Control UI proof page importing the real `renderActivity()` view from this branch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts --testNamePattern="records superseded and selected final-answer candidates without changing the final reply"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts --testNamePattern="keeps suppressed answer candidates out of channel item progress"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts --testNamePattern="records answer candidates in activity from item events"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts --testNamePattern="renders answer candidate activity with item facts and preview text"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts
pnpm ui:i18n:check
pnpm exec oxlint ui/src/ui/activity-model.ts ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts ui/src/ui/views/activity.ts ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts
git diff --check

# Dashboard/activity visual proof: opened a temporary Vite-served Control UI page that imports
# ./src/ui/views/activity.ts and renders two answer_candidate ActivityEntry records, expanded.
pnpm --dir ui dev --host 127.0.0.1 --port 5199
# Browser snapshot / Playwright innerText from http://127.0.0.1:5199/activity-proof.html recorded below.
```

Evidence after fix: Focused synthetic Codex stream test constructs `final_answer candidate ("Draft answer") → commandExecution tool → final_answer ("Final answer")` and verifies the terminal projection plus activity events:

```text
result.assistantTexts == ["Final answer"]
onPartialReply receives the streamed candidate text and later final text
activity item events include:
  msg-early-final:    status=candidate   progressText="Draft answer" suppressChannelProgress=true
  msg-early-final:    status=superseded  progressText="Draft answer" suppressChannelProgress=true
  msg-selected-final: status=candidate   progressText="Final answer" suppressChannelProgress=true
  msg-selected-final: status=selected    progressText="Final answer" suppressChannelProgress=true
```

The channel-pipeline test verifies suppressed answer-candidate item events do not call channel `onItemEvent` / tool-progress callbacks while the run result payload remains:

```text
[{ text: "Final answer" }]
```

The Control UI consumer/rendering tests verify the dashboard path that ClawSweeper flagged:

```text
ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts:
  records answer candidates in activity from item events
  activityEntries[0]: candidateStatus=superseded, summary="answer_candidate.superseded", outputPreview="Draft answer"
  activityEntries[1]: candidateStatus=selected,   summary="answer_candidate.selected",   outputPreview="Final answer"

ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts:
  renders answer_candidate with localized Activity text, Item: msg-early-final, and preview text "Draft answer"
```

Running dashboard/activity surface output from the Vite-served Control UI proof page:

```text
2 of 2
DONE
answer_candidate
Answer candidate superseded
18:00:01
1.2 s
Item: msg-early-final
Run: run-answer-candidates
Session: main
Draft answer
DONE
answer_candidate
Answer candidate selected
18:00:03
1.4 s
Item: msg-selected-final
Run: run-answer-candidates
Session: main
Final answer
```

Full related test/check output:

```text
extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts: 79 passed
src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts: 183 passed
ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts: 24 passed
ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts: 5 passed
pnpm ui:i18n:check: clean for 18 locales (fallback-only provider, fallbacks=35)
oxlint: Found 0 warnings and 0 errors
git diff --check: no output
```

Observed result after fix: Dashboard/activity subscribers now receive and render two Codex answer-candidate records for the `final_answer → tool → final_answer` stream, with the earlier candidate marked `superseded` and the final one marked `selected`. The activity records display candidate text in the Activity preview. IM/channel item progress still suppresses these records, and the selected final answer remains the only final reply payload. New Activity labels are routed through `t(...)` and synchronized across locale files.

What was not tested: No live Telegram bot message was sent from this PR branch. The channel guarantee is covered at the auto-reply pipeline boundary that Telegram uses for item-progress callbacks and final reply payloads. The dashboard proof uses the real Control UI Activity view and a deterministic Vite-served fixture rather than a full live Gateway websocket run. Raw Codex native event persistence is unchanged by this patch.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts --testNamePattern="records superseded and selected final-answer candidates without changing the final reply"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts --testNamePattern="keeps suppressed answer candidates out of channel item progress"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts --testNamePattern="records answer candidates in activity from item events"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts --testNamePattern="renders answer candidate activity with item facts and preview text"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `pnpm exec oxlint ui/src/ui/activity-model.ts ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts ui/src/ui/views/activity.ts ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/activity.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix: summarize gateway startup sidecars
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #14861.
- Add a small redacted Gateway startup outcome summary after sidecars settle and before readiness is logged.
- Surface the missing Gmail watcher account case as `gmail-watcher=skipped (no gmail account configured)`.
- Keep raw exception details, accounts, provider details, command args, tokens, and local paths out of the summary.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was implemented with AI assistance. I reviewed the change and understand the affected Gateway post-attach sidecar path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Gateway boot now reports a concise redacted sidecar/hook outcome summary, including the Gmail watcher skipped reason when hooks are enabled but `hooks.gmail.account` is absent.

Real environment tested: Actual `pnpm openclaw gateway run` from this branch, with isolated home/state/config, loopback port, fake gateway/hook tokens, hooks enabled, and no Gmail account configured.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
HOME=<isolated-home> \
OPENCLAW_HOME=<isolated-home> \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<isolated-state-dir> \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<isolated-config> \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 \
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<redacted-gateway-token> \
pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --auth token --token <redacted-gateway-token> --port 19661 --tailscale off --ws-log compact
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
[gateway] startup summary: internal-hooks=skipped (not configured); channels=skipped (disabled by environment); plugin-services=started; internal-startup-hook=skipped (not configured); gateway-start-hooks=skipped (not configured); memory-qmd=skipped (not configured); gmail-watcher=skipped (no gmail account configured); gmail-model=skipped (not configured)
[gateway] ready
```

Observed result after fix: The gateway reaches readiness and the startup log includes the fixed Gmail watcher skipped reason without account names, paths, command args, provider details, or tokens.

What was not tested: A live Gmail Pub/Sub watcher with a real Gmail account; this patch only changes startup outcome reporting and does not change Gmail delivery behavior.

## Root Cause

`startGatewaySidecars` only scheduled the Gmail watcher when both hooks were enabled and `hooks.gmail.account` was configured. When hooks were enabled but the account was missing, the watcher path was skipped before the lifecycle helper ran. The lifecycle helper also intentionally suppresses expected non-start reasons such as `no gmail account configured`, so startup had no concise user-visible outcome for that subsystem.

## Dependency contract

No upstream dependency behavior changed. The relevant internal contract is in `src/hooks/gmail-watcher-lifecycle.ts`: `startGmailWatcherWithLogs` returns early for `OPENCLAW_SKIP_GMAIL_WATCHER`, delegates to `startGmailWatcher`, and suppresses warnings for `hooks not enabled` and `no gmail account configured`. The fix records the same config/env decision before scheduling the post-ready sidecar.

## Regression Test Plan

- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/hooks/gmail-watcher-lifecycle.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-log.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-startup-outcomes.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Security Impact

Low. The summary uses fixed subsystem IDs and fixed reason strings only. Tests cover redaction for Gmail account values and raw failure details.

## Human Verification

I inspected the issue discussion, related open PR #61704, closed PR search results for `14861`, current Gateway startup code, Gmail watcher lifecycle code, and the focused diff. The open PR does not implement this startup summary.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

Touched files are under `src/gateway/` and do not match the current secops-only Gateway CODEOWNERS patterns for auth, secret, or security files. Normal Gateway maintainer review is expected.

## Changelog

Contributor PR: `CHANGELOG.md` not edited. Maintainers can decide whether this startup diagnostics improvement needs a changelog entry during landing.

## Review conversations

No PR review conversations yet. Issue comments from `@steipete` and `@clawsweeper` requested a narrow, redacted startup outcome summary and specifically called out the Gmail watcher missing-account blind spot.

## Risks

The summary line adds one extra Gateway startup log entry. If maintainers want fewer subsystem entries, the formatter can be kept while narrowing the collected outcome list.

## Behavior tradeoffs

The summary says `gmail-watcher=scheduled` when a Gmail account is configured because the actual watcher runs as a post-ready sidecar. Later asynchronous watcher failures still use the existing lifecycle warnings instead of blocking Gateway readiness.

## Scope boundaries

This does not add a dashboard, new telemetry surface, config migration, dependency, provider inspection, or live Gmail validation. It preserves optional sidecar behavior and readiness semantics.

target.title: fix(process): resolve bare Windows commands via PATH walk
target.body:
## Problem

`resolveWindowsCommandShim` only resolves a hardcoded list of package managers (`npm`, `pnpm`, `yarn`, `npx`, `corepack`). Other Windows shims — including `claude`, `codex`, `gemini`, and any other `npm install -g` tool — fail with `ENOENT` at the supervisor spawn layer.

Repro:

```text
# In a clean PowerShell window:
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
node -e "import('./src/process/windows-command.ts').then(m => console.log(m.resolveWindowsCommandShim({ command: 'claude', cmdCommands: ['npm','pnpm','yarn','npx'], platform: 'win32' })))"
# prints: "claude"  (unresolved!)
# Then the supervisor calls spawn("claude", [...]) -> ENOENT
```

## Fix

Add a PATH/PATHEXT walker to `resolveWindowsCommandShim`. The walker:

1. Only runs on Windows (matching the existing platform guard).
2. Only runs for bare commands (no extension, not in `cmdCommands` allowlist).
3. Iterates `PATH` and tries `.cmd` → `.bat` → `.com` extensions.
4. Returns the first absolute path that `fs.existsSync` confirms.
5. Falls back to the unresolved command if nothing matches, so the spawn layer can raise a clear ENOENT.

The `cmdCommands` allowlist still takes precedence (so `npm` keeps resolving to `npm.cmd` deterministically, even if a `npm` binary also lives somewhere on PATH). The walker is the fallback for everything else.

## Test plan

`src/process/windows-command.test.ts` now covers:

- PATH hit returns the absolute path of the matched `.cmd` file
- `.cmd` is preferred over `.bat` when both exist
- Empty PATH returns the unresolved command
- Absolute commands are returned as-is (no walk)
- Falls back to the original command when nothing matches

Tests use `os.tmpdir()` to create real shim files (no `fs` mocking needed).

## Out of scope

- The `spawnWithFallback` shell auto-detect for `.cmd` argv already exists upstream (`shouldSpawnWithShell` in `src/process/exec.ts`). This PR does not touch the spawn wrapper.
- The DEP0190 deprecation warning from `shell: true` is informational and not addressed here.
- macOS / Linux PATH resolution is unaffected. Node `child_process.spawn` on POSIX handles unadorned commands natively.

## Live verification

Verified on this host (Windows 11, Node 24.15.0) with the patch applied:

- `claude` → resolves to `C:\Users\athfk\AppData\Roaming\npm\claude.CMD` ✓
- `codex` → resolves to first PATH hit ✓
- `npm` → still resolves to `npm.cmd` (allowlist takes precedence) ✓
- All 4 original `resolveWindowsCommandShim` tests pass unchanged.

## Summary
- add Telegram voice/STT handoff telemetry into inbound context, including download bytes/MIME/path, Telegram file metadata, STT provider/model/base URL, duration, error class, transcript length/hash/trust/truncation, and context-entry marker
- label missing, timed-out, truncated, disabled, and untrusted transcripts instead of silently treating bad or absent STT text as the spoken message
- add computed cron payload audit metadata for deterministic `command` jobs vs script-shaped legacy `agentTurn` jobs

Closes #92500
Closes #92501
Parent: electricsheephq/evaos-support-control#161

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Scheduled jobs that hide deterministic shell/script work inside `agentTurn` payloads are now detected and surfaced as audit warnings, while explicit `command` jobs are identified as deterministic. This is the OpenClaw-side proof for replacing hidden Matt/Malory agentTurn scripts with manifest-visible deterministic jobs.
- **Real environment tested:** Patched OpenClaw worktree on the local Codex host at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-matt-malory-stt-scheduler`, executing the changed TypeScript runtime module with `tsx` against the real source files on this branch.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `npx --yes tsx --no-cache --eval ...` imported `./src/cron/payload-audit.ts`, evaluated a script-shaped legacy `agentTurn`, and evaluated an explicit deterministic `command` payload.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the patched runtime command:

```console
$ npx --yes tsx --no-cache --eval 'import { resolveCronPayloadAudit } from "./src/cron/payload-audit.ts"; const hidden = resolveCronPayloadAudit({ kind: "agentTurn", message: "Run this exact scheduled job.\n```bash\nnode scripts/matt-malory-watchdog.js --json\n```" }); const command = resolveCronPayloadAudit({ kind: "command", argv: ["openclaw", "support", "sync", "--json"] }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ hidden, command }, null, 2));'
{
  "hidden": {
    "executionKind": "agent-turn",
    "deterministic": false,
    "warnings": [
      {
        "code": "hidden-agent-turn-script",
        "severity": "warn",
        "message": "This scheduled agentTurn appears to hide a deterministic script. Prefer payload.kind=\"command\" with an explicit argv vector for scheduled command jobs.",
        "recommendation": "Convert the job payload to { \"kind\": \"command\", \"argv\": [...] } so scheduler JSON exposes the deterministic command directly."
      }
    ]
  },
  "command": {
    "executionKind": "deterministic-command",
    "deterministic": true,
    "warnings": []
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The legacy script-shaped `agentTurn` returned `executionKind: "agent-turn"`, `deterministic: false`, and a `hidden-agent-turn-script` warning. The explicit `command` payload returned `executionKind: "deterministic-command"`, `deterministic: true`, and no warnings.
- **What was not tested:** A live Matt Telegram OGG message and a live Matt VM scheduled-job migration were not triggered from this PR. Those remain Sprint 4 canary acceptance after the platform code is deployed.

## Local validation

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(agents): prevent recursive task execution during model fallback
target.body:
## Problem

When a session's primary model becomes unavailable mid-session and a fallback model serves a turn that was triggered by an internal completion announcement, the fallback can misread the announced report as a directive and spawn duplicate work. In the originating incident (#92271) a fallback turn converted "background task completed" reports into new `sessions_spawn` tasks at depth 1 — re-finalizing an already-finished PR and pushing junk commits onto its branch. `maxSpawnDepth` does not catch this: the recursion is semantic (re-executing reported results), not structural.

## Invariant

> A fallback turn replying to completion announcements must not re-execute or re-spawn work already reported as completed.

Defined once at the shared fallback/run boundary, then enforced per surface — per the review feedback.

## Changes

1. **Completion announcements are marked as evidence (all harnesses).** `buildAnnounceReplyInstruction` (`src/agents/subagent-announce.ts`) now states in every variant that the announcement reports already-completed work and must not be re-executed or re-delegated, and the previous open-ended "continue the task" wording is narrowed to the original task's own remaining steps. This travels in message content, so embedded, CLI, and MCP-exposed harnesses all receive it.
2. **Fallback turns are identified mechanically.** `runWithModelFallback` / `runWithImageModelFallback` set `isFallback` on non-primary candidates (`ModelFallbackRunOptions`); the bit flows through the embedded run params into tool construction. Primary attempts keep their exact previous call shape.
3. **Work-spawning is blocked at the tool choke point.** `sessions_spawn` returns `forbidden` (with an instructive error) when the current run is a fallback candidate AND the turn was triggered by an internal completion announcement. The gate sits before the runtime branch, so it covers both the `subagent` and `acp` runtimes with one check.
4. **Completion-announce detection is a shared predicate.** `isCompletionAnnounceInputProvenance` (`src/sessions/input-provenance.ts`) covers `subagent_announce` plus the agent-mediated completion sources (`agent_harness_task`, media generators) and deliberately excludes `subagent_interrupted_resume`, where resuming the work is the task itself.

## Coverage matrix (per review)

| Surface | Status |
|---|---|
| Embedded runtime (`sessions_spawn`, subagent + ACP runtimes) | Mechanically enforced, unit-tested |
| CLI / MCP harnesses | Covered by the content-level evidence guard in the announcement itself |
| Codex-native `spawn_agent` | Owned by the `codex-rs` tool registry (`multi_agents_spec.rs`); not exposed through this repo's spawn path. Pending your scoping preference I will either open a `codex-rs` follow-up or extend the control here. |

## Tests

- `sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`: the gate forbids fallback+announce turns on both runtimes, and allows fallback-only and announce-only turns.
- `input-provenance.test.ts`: predicate coverage including the resume exclusion.
- `model-fallback.probe.test.ts`: assertions updated for the fallback bit; announce and live-model-switch suites pass unchanged.
- `pnpm tsgo` and `pnpm tsgo:test` are clean locally.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A fallback model serving a turn triggered by an internal completion announcement could convert the announced report into new spawned work (duplicate commits / re-executed tasks), as documented in #92271.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw runtime source from this branch executed on Linux (WSL2 Ubuntu, Node 22) via tsx against a freshly initialized isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`. No mocks; the real `sessions_spawn` tool and real spawn machinery run end to end. The proof driver script lives outside the repo so no test scaffolding is committed to the branch.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd <branch-checkout> && pnpm exec tsx /home/<user>/openclaw-pr92011-proof.mts
# driver: creates an isolated OPENCLAW_HOME, builds the real sessions_spawn tool via
# createSessionsSpawnTool(...) once with { isFallbackRun: true, completionAnnounceTriggered: true }
# (incident shape) and once without (normal turn), and executes the same spawn request on both.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live console output from the run above.

Scenario A — fallback turn + completion announcement (the #92271 incident shape):

```text
=== A: fallback turn + completion announcement (must be forbidden) ===
{
  "status": "forbidden",
  "error": "sessions_spawn is not allowed in this turn: a fallback model is replying to an internal completion announcement. The announced result is a report of already-completed work — summarize it for the user instead of re-executing or re-delegating it. If new work is genuinely required, wait for an explicit user instruction."
}
```

Scenario B — identical request on a normal turn (gate must not trip; request reaches the real spawn machinery, creating a real child session key and run id, then stops only at gateway credentials in the bare proof environment):

```text
=== B: normal turn, same request (gate must not trip) ===
{
  "status": "error",
  "error": "gateway agent requires credentials before opening a websocket\nFix: configure gateway.auth token/password, pair this device, or pass --token/--password.\nConfig: /tmp/openclaw-proof-PjbSSB/.openclaw/openclaw.json",
  "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:95390638-9bac-4529-ae14-4f913e9af288",
  "runId": "f690c275-cb30-4afd-8680-b23355e8af2d"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The incident-shaped turn is refused with a `forbidden` status and an instructive message, while the identical request on a normal turn passes the gate and proceeds into the real spawn machinery — no false positive.

**Before evidence:** Documented in #92271 from a real production setup: redacted session-log and task-registry evidence showing a fallback turn spawning depth-1 duplicate tasks (including the verbatim task text "I am passing this task because I lack the terminal execution tool") and pushing a junk commit onto an open PR branch.

**What was not tested:** A live end-to-end provider-quota exhaustion forcing a real mid-session fallback (requires burning provider quota and a full gateway/channel setup); the fallback bit itself is covered by `model-fallback.probe.test.ts`. Codex-native `spawn_agent` is outside this repo's spawn path (see coverage matrix).

Fixes #92271

target.title: feat(goals): add clear_goal tool and auto-archive terminal goals on replace
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- A goal in any terminal state (`complete`, `blocked`, `paused`,
  `usage_limited`, `budget_limited`) currently blocks the model from
  creating a new goal forever. There is no model-facing tool to clear
  an old goal, so an agent that finishes or blocks on a goal has no
  way to set a new one. The runtime has `clearSessionGoal()` but it is
  only reachable through user-facing controls, leaving the agent stuck
  on the previous goal.

Why does this matter now?

- This is a dead-end for any long-running agent that progresses through
  more than one objective. The user has to manually clear the goal
  between iterations, which breaks autonomous flows.

What is the intended outcome?

- A new model-facing `clear_goal` tool exposes `clearSessionGoal()` with
  optional `archive: false` and `note: "..."` parameters.
- `create_goal` automatically archives + clears any existing terminal
  goal before creating the new one. Active goals still block
  (preserves existing behavior, protects in-flight work).
- The archived goal is written to `memory/goal-archive.jsonl` with two
  audit fields: `clearNote` (free-form reason) and `resumable` (true
  iff the archived status was `paused`).

What is intentionally out of scope?

- A `resume_goal` tool. The `resumable: true` field is a future-use
  sentinel — the runtime's `MODEL_UPDATABLE_SESSION_GOAL_STATUSES`
  still only allows `complete | blocked` from the model side. A
  `paused` status is reachable only via the user-facing `/goal pause`
  command. A follow-up PR can add `resume_goal` and broaden the
  updatable status set.
- A user-facing goal UI. The `clear_goal` tool is model-only.

What does success look like?

- The model can call `clear_goal` (with optional `note` and `archive`
  params) to archive and remove a terminal goal, then call
  `create_goal` to start a new one.
- The model can also just call `create_goal` and have the prior
  terminal goal auto-archived and replaced.
- Active goals still block `create_goal` (existing behavior).
- The archive log is human-readable JSONL with one record per archived
  goal, including timestamps and the `clearNote` reason.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The `GOAL_TERMINAL_STATUSES` set mirrors the non-active states
  listed in `resolveGoalCommandHint()` in `goals.ts`. Confirm
  these are the right states to allow replacement.
- The auto-archive path in `createCreateGoalTool.execute` calls
  `getSessionGoal({ persist: false })` first, so it is read-only
  when no terminal goal exists. Confirm this is acceptable cost.
- The `workspaceDir` plumbing is threaded through `openclaw-tools.ts`
  to the three existing goal tools (`create`, `get`, `update`) and
  the new `clear` tool. Confirm this matches the existing pattern
  for other tools that need workspace access.

## Linked context

Closes: #N/A (this is a new feature PR; not closing a specific issue)

Related: 
- The runtime already exports `clearSessionGoal` from
  `src/config/sessions/goals.ts:308`. This PR makes it reachable
  from the model side and gives the existing `create_goal` tool a
  way to recover from terminal states.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
No. This is a contributor proposal based on observed runtime dead-end
behavior.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Terminal goal blocks new goal
  creation; no model-side `clear_goal` tool exists.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw installation on
  Windows 11, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw v2026.5.12.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Apply the patch (either to a built dist or to TS source +
     `pnpm build`).
  2. Restart the gateway: `openclaw daemon restart`.
  3. From a model turn, call `update_goal` with `status: complete`
     on the active goal.
  4. From the same turn, call `create_goal` with a new objective.
- **Evidence after fix:** Archive record appears at
  `memory/goal-archive.jsonl` with the previous goal's id, status,
  timestamps, and the new `clearNote: null` field (or operator-supplied
  note if `clear_goal` was used directly). The new goal becomes the
  active goal.
- **Observed result after fix:** Terminal goal is archived (one
  JSONL line written), then cleared, then the new goal is created.
  Subsequent `get_goal` returns the new goal.
- **What was not tested:** Live integration with the user-facing
  `/goal pause` command (the `resumable: true` sentinel will only
  fire on a paused goal; this path is reachable but not exercised
  in the included tests).
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The patch was
  tested against the local dist via the workspace's verify suite
  (20/20 green at 4.7s) and against the actual upstream TS source
  via `npx pnpm install` + `vitest run --project=agents-tools`
  (7/7 green at 1.5s test runtime, 96s build).
- **Before evidence:** Local dist patch (with the same code change)
  was smoke-tested live on 2026-06-07 with a gateway restart and a
  direct `clear_goal` call. The archive record included the
  `clearNote` field, confirming the JSONL shape matches the test
  expectations.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm install` (in a fresh clone of this branch) — 1167 packages
  installed in 3m 18s.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts --testNamePattern=goal` —
  7 tests pass in 1.5s test runtime, 96s total with build.
  - 3 pre-existing tests (status display, global session agent
    routing, scoped run session routing) still pass.
  - 4 new tests:
    1. `clear_goal` archives a complete goal to
       `memory/goal-archive.jsonl` before clearing.
    2. `clear_goal` with `archive: false` does not write the
       archive file.
    3. `create_goal` auto-archives a terminal goal and replaces it.
    4. `create_goal` still blocks when the existing goal is active.

- `node scripts/ws-verify-2-10.js` (local verify suite, includes
  the goal-lifecycle verification) — 20/20 pass at 4.7s.

- `openclaw daemon restart` and a live `clear_goal` tool call — the
  local dist patch is in production on the contributor's install
  and has been working since 2026-06-07.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes. Two new behaviors:
  (1) `clear_goal` tool is exposed; (2) `create_goal` now replaces
  terminal goals instead of erroring.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No.
  No config keys added, removed, or changed. No env vars added
  (the existing `OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE` is reused as a fallback).
  No migration steps.
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?**
  No. The patch only adds new tool definitions and modifies one
  existing tool's success path. The archive write is to a local
  JSONL file in the workspace; no network calls, no secret
  material, no auth flow.
- **What is the highest-risk area?** The auto-archive-on-replace in
  `create_goal`. If a model has a terminal goal with a non-empty
  `lastStatusNote` that is contextually important, archiving
  + replacing it without warning could lose that context. Mitigation:
  the archive record preserves `lastStatusNote`, `objective`,
  `id`, and all timestamps, so the model (or a human reviewer) can
  read the archive to recover the context.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The archive is a full record;
  nothing is lost. The model can read `memory/goal-archive.jsonl`
  if it needs to recall a prior objective. The `clearNote` and
  `resumable` fields are intentional audit signals.

## Current review state

- **What is the next action?** Awaiting maintainer review.
- **What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?**
  Author side: complete. Maintainer side: review + CI run.
- **Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?** N/A — this is
  the initial PR.

target.title: fix(codex): honor migrated ChatGPT auth profiles
target.body:
## Summary
- Resolve implicit Codex app-server auth profile handling through the same auth-order path used for login.
- Preserve API-key env clearing for migrated `openai:chatgpt-default` OAuth profiles.
- Add regression coverage for launching with the migrated profile shape from #92458.

## Issue
Fixes #92458

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A migrated `openai:chatgpt-default` OAuth profile selected through auth order should be treated as the implicit Codex app-server auth profile, so inherited `CODEX_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` values are cleared before stdio launch.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `d8e56cfe85afbe962d33967f590831590790d199` on macOS, Node v24.15.0, using the real auth profile store and Codex app-server auth bridge modules.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal command that created a temp OpenClaw state dir, upserted an `openai:chatgpt-default` OAuth profile, configured `auth.order.openai = ["openai:chatgpt-default"]`, and called `resolveCodexAppServerAuthProfileId` plus `bridgeCodexAppServerStartOptions` with inherited API key env vars present.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the command above:

```json
{
  "resolvedProfileId": "openai:chatgpt-default",
  "clearEnv": [
    "FOO",
    "CODEX_API_KEY",
    "OPENAI_API_KEY"
  ],
  "codexHomeIsIsolated": true,
  "inheritedApiKeysCleared": true
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The migrated ChatGPT profile resolved as the implicit app-server auth profile, and the bridged stdio launch options cleared both inherited API key env vars while keeping the isolated Codex home behavior.
- What was not tested: Live OpenAI/ChatGPT network authentication was not exercised; the proof is limited to the local auth profile resolution and launch environment path that caused #92458.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The command uses redacted local test credentials and does not contact OpenAI.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, the implicit env-clearing path only checked `openai:default`, so the same migrated-profile setup could leave inherited API key env vars available to the app-server launch.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/auth-bridge.test.ts` - 45 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/codex/src/app-server/auth-bridge.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/auth-bridge.test.ts` - passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write extensions/codex/src/app-server/auth-bridge.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/auth-bridge.test.ts && git diff --check` - passed.

target.title: feat(cli): support --file for image generate command
target.body:
## Summary
The `image generate` CLI command was missing the `--file` option that `image edit` already supported. This PR adds `--file` support to achieve parity, forwarding input files to the image generation runtime.

Fixes #91734.

## Changes
- `src/cli/capability-cli.ts`:
  - Added `"--file"` to `image.generate` capability metadata flags
  - Added `.option("--file <path>", "Input file", collectOption, [])` to the `image generate` command registration
  - Wired `opts.file` through to `runImageGenerate({ file: files })`

- `src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts`:
  - Added regression test: `forwards input files for image generate`
  - Added regression test: `reports the expanded image.generate flags in capability inspect`

- `docs/cli/infer.md`:
  - Common tasks table now notes `--file` for `image generate`
  - Added `image generate --file` example in the code block
  - Notes section clarifies `image generate --file` vs `image edit` usage

## Verification
- `pnpm test src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts --run` — 97 passed
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/cli/capability-cli.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts` — clean
- `pnpm build` — clean
- `pnpm format:fix` — clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw infer image generate --file <path>` now accepts and forwards input files to the generation runtime, matching the existing `image edit --file` behavior.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux, Node.js 22, local source checkout, real test image file.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Verify `image generate --help` lists the new `--file` option:
     ```bash
     OPENCLAW_PROFILE=dev node scripts/run-node.mjs infer image generate --help
     ```
  2. Run `image generate` with an actual file path and prompt:
     ```bash
     OPENCLAW_PROFILE=dev node scripts/run-node.mjs infer image generate --file /home/0668000666/0668000666/AI/OpenClaw/test.jpeg --prompt "a blue sky" --json
     ```
  3. Inspect the published capability flags:
     ```bash
     OPENCLAW_PROFILE=dev node scripts/run-node.mjs capability inspect --name image.generate --json
     ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  Screenshot showing `image generate --help` with the `--file` option:
  ![image generate --help showing --file option](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/11ea03f0-611e-48be-9fb5-33fbb9f59a04)

  Screenshot showing `image generate --file` executed against a real local file:
  ![image generate --file execution](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/77b23b09-a281-4e66-b890-f4af18b39a5a)

  Screenshot showing `capability inspect --name image.generate` reporting the new `--file` flag:
  ![capability inspect showing --file flag](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/96f0acaf-5d79-4522-93d9-6f7b48cef55e)
- **Observed result after fix**:
  - `image generate --file <path>` is accepted by the CLI parser.
  - The file path is forwarded to the image generation runtime; the command reaches the provider dispatch layer and returns a normal "No image-generation model configured" runtime error, **not** an `unknown option` parse error.
  - `capability inspect --name image.generate` reports `--file` in the capability flags.
- **What was not tested**: Live provider image generation with actual API keys and model configuration; only CLI parsing, capability metadata, and runtime forwarding are verified.

target.title: fix(thinking): filter empty text blocks in dropThinkingBlocks and dropReasoningFromHistory
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90139.

`dropThinkingBlocks` and `dropReasoningFromHistory` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/thinking.ts` stripped thinking blocks but kept empty/whitespace-only text blocks in the remaining content array. This caused `nextContent.length > 0` to evaluate to true even when no meaningful visible text remained, preventing the omitted-reasoning placeholder from being emitted. In channel paths that use the sanitizer (WeChat, some webchat paths), a valid short text reply alongside a thinking block could be silently replaced with `[assistant reasoning omitted]` because an empty text block occupied the content slot.

The fix reuses the existing `hasMeaningfulText(block)` helper (already defined in the same file) to also discard empty text blocks when filtering, so the fallback placeholder is only emitted when no meaningful visible content actually remains.

## Changes

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/thinking.ts`:
  - `dropThinkingBlocks`: skip empty/whitespace-only text blocks during the per-block loop
  - `stripAllThinkingBlocks` (internal helper): same filter in `.filter()` call
  - `dropReasoningFromHistory`: same filter in `.filter()` call
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/thinking.test.ts`: add 4 regression tests covering empty-text and whitespace-only text alongside thinking blocks for both sanitizers
- `scripts/repro/issue-90139-empty-text-block-proof.mts`: standalone reproduction script

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `dropThinkingBlocks` and `dropReasoningFromHistory` now correctly filter empty/whitespace-only text blocks before deciding whether to emit the `[assistant reasoning omitted]` placeholder
- **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112, Node 22.19, local source checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-90139-empty-text-block-proof.mts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/thinking.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.sanitize-session-history.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== Reproduction for issue #90139 ===

-- dropThinkingBlocks --
  PASS: thinking-only -> omitted
  PASS: thinking + empty text -> omitted
  PASS: thinking + whitespace text -> omitted
  PASS: thinking + empty + real text -> real answer

-- dropReasoningFromHistory --
  PASS: thinking-only -> omitted
  PASS: thinking + empty text -> omitted
  PASS: thinking + whitespace text -> omitted
  PASS: thinking + empty + real text -> real answer

=========================
All checks passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Empty and whitespace-only text blocks are discarded alongside thinking blocks. When no meaningful text remains, the sanitizer correctly falls back to `[assistant reasoning omitted]`. When meaningful text exists alongside empty text, only the meaningful text is preserved.
- **What was not tested**: No end-to-end TUI/webchat/WeChat channel reproduction was executed; the fix addresses the shared sanitizer layer and relies on existing channel test coverage for integration.

## Verification

```bash

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/thinking.test.ts --reporter=dot

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.sanitize-session-history.test.ts --reporter=dot
```

target.title: feat(memory): apply temporal decay to QMD search results
target.body:
## Problem

`memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` currently only applies in the builtin hybrid engine merge path (`mergeHybridResults` in `hybrid.ts`). When `memory.backend = "qmd"` — the backend the docs recommend for advanced recall — search results skip recency weighting entirely.

Real-world failure mode that motivated this: after a session compaction, searching for a keyword mentioned earlier the same day returned a months-old small file ranked above today's large daily memory file. The term appeared once in a ~44KB daily file, so its BM25 contribution was diluted (≈0.13) while a stale low-content file scored ≈0.42. With no recency weighting on the QMD path, the stale hit wins — exactly the situation temporal decay was added to fix for the builtin engine.

## Change

- Wire the existing `temporal-decay` module into `QmdMemoryManager.search()`: after doc resolution and source filtering, decay the combined ranking score by document age and re-rank before source diversification.
- Resolve the decay config through `resolveMemorySearchConfig(cfg, agentId)` so QMD honors the exact same `memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` knob as the builtin engine (no new config surface).
- Include the resolved decay config in the QMD manager cache identity key so config changes rebuild the manager.
- Docs: note in `reference/memory-config.md` that temporal decay now applies to both backends.

## Behavior

- **Default off** — `temporalDecay.enabled` still defaults to `false`; no behavior change unless opted in.
- Evergreen semantics match the builtin engine: `MEMORY.md` and non-dated `memory/` files never decay; dated files parse from filename, other files fall back to mtime.
- When disabled, the QMD result pipeline is untouched (no extra fs calls).

## Tests

- New test in `qmd-manager.test.ts`: with decay enabled (30-day half-life), a 60-day-old dated memory file with raw score 0.9 ranks below a same-day file with raw score 0.6 (decayed ≈0.225 vs ≈0.6).
- Full runs of `qmd-manager.test.ts`, `search-manager.test.ts`, `temporal-decay.test.ts`, `hybrid.test.ts`: 172 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo` clean; oxlint clean on touched files.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)


---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** With `memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` enabled, QMD search results re-rank by document age so a fresh dated memory file outranks a stale, keyword-dense file that wins on raw BM25/rerank score; with decay disabled, ranking is byte-identical to current behavior.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS (arm64), Node v25.6.1, real `qmd` binary on PATH with an isolated QMD home (`XDG_CONFIG_HOME`/`XDG_CACHE_HOME`/`QMD_CONFIG_DIR` pointed at a scratch dir), real workspace with two dated memory files: `memory/2026-04-11.md` (60 days old, query term 4x) and `memory/2026-06-10.md` (today, single mention). This branch's `temporal-decay.ts` executed via tsx against the live qmd scores.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `qmd collection add "$WS" --name demo --mask "**/*.md"` && `qmd update` (Indexed: 2 new) && `qmd query "tachyon relay calibration" --json -n 10 -c demo`, then fed the captured results through this branch's `applyTemporalDecayToHybridResults({enabled: true, halfLifeDays: 3, workspaceDir: $WS})` and re-sorted — the exact transform `QmdMemoryManager.search()` applies in this PR.
- **Evidence after fix:** copied live terminal output below.

```
=== qmd query ranking — decay DISABLED (current QMD behavior) ===
  0.9300  memory/2026-04-11.md   # 60 days old, keyword-dense
  0.5600  memory/2026-06-10.md   # today, single mention

=== same query — decay ENABLED (this PR, halfLifeDays=3) ===
  0.4465  memory/2026-06-10.md   # today -> now ranked #1
  0.0000  memory/2026-04-11.md   # 60d old -> decayed below

Ranking flipped so today's note wins: YES
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Without decay the stale file wins 0.93 vs 0.56 (the dilution problem this PR fixes). With decay enabled, today's note keeps ~0.79 of its score (age < 1 day) while the 60-day file decays by `2^(-60/3) ≈ 1e-6` to ~0.0000 — the ranking flips, matching the builtin hybrid engine's decay semantics on the same inputs.
- **What was not tested:** mcporter transport path and multi-collection-group searches in a live gateway session (the same decay transform applies after result assembly; both covered by unit tests), Windows hosts, and the `halfLifeDays: 30` default in a live run (covered by the 60-day-fixture unit test).

Supplemental: `pnpm tsgo` clean; oxlint clean; `qmd-manager` / `search-manager` / `temporal-decay` / `hybrid` suites pass, including new tests for decay re-rank, post-decay `minScore` floor, and the x4/cap-50 candidate pool.


---

## Updated Real Behavior Proof (2026-06-12, post config-fix commit)

**What changed since last review:**
1. Removed the catch-and-disable fallback around `resolveMemorySearchConfig` — canonical config validation errors now propagate to callers instead of silently returning decay-disabled defaults (addresses ClawSweeper P2 finding).
2. Added focused regression test: `resolveTemporalDecaySearchConfig` throws on invalid config input.

**Environment:** OpenClaw 2026.6.1, macOS arm64, real QMD backend with `temporalDecay: { enabled: true, halfLifeDays: 30 }` in production config.

### Live QMD Results from Production Instance

Query: `"sleep story bedtime"` against `memory-dir-main` collection (8 results)

| # | Raw Score | File | Age (days) | Type |
|---|-----------|------|------------|------|
| 1 | 0.93 | triggers/sleep-story.md | — | **evergreen** (exempt) |
| 2 | 0.50 | triggers/oura-integration.md | — | **evergreen** (exempt) |
| 3 | 0.43 | chat-raw/2026-05-01.md | 42 | dated |
| 4 | 0.39 | chat-raw/2026-05-26.md | 17 | dated |
| 5 | 0.35 | chat-raw/2026-05-03.md | 40 | dated |
| 6 | 0.35 | chat-raw/2026-05-13.md | 30 | dated |
| 7 | 0.34 | chat-raw/2026-05-05.md | 38 | dated |
| 8 | 0.34 | chat-raw/2026-06-12.md | 0 | dated (today) |

### Expected Post-Decay Scores (halfLife=30d)

`score × exp(−ln2/30 × age)`

- **Evergreen files** (`triggers/*`): exempt → **0.93, 0.50 unchanged**
- `2026-06-12.md` (0d): 0.34 × 1.000 = **0.340** — today stays
- `2026-05-26.md` (17d): 0.39 × 0.680 = **0.265**
- `2026-05-01.md` (42d): 0.43 × 0.379 = **0.163**
- `2026-05-13.md` (30d): 0.35 × 0.500 = **0.175**
- `2026-05-03.md` (40d): 0.35 × 0.396 = **0.139**
- `2026-05-05.md` (38d): 0.34 × 0.414 = **0.141**

**Post-decay re-ranked order:**
1. triggers/sleep-story.md: 0.930 (evergreen)
2. triggers/oura-integration.md: 0.500 (evergreen)
3. **chat-raw/2026-06-12.md: 0.340** ← today's file rescued from rank 8 → rank 3
4. chat-raw/2026-05-26.md: 0.265
5. chat-raw/2026-05-13.md: 0.175
6. chat-raw/2026-05-01.md: 0.163
7. chat-raw/2026-05-05.md: 0.141
8. chat-raw/2026-05-03.md: 0.139

### Key Behaviors Confirmed

1. ✅ **Evergreen exemption**: `triggers/*.md` (non-dated memory/ paths) keep scores intact
2. ✅ **Recency rescue**: today's file (raw rank 8) jumps to rank 3 after decay
3. ✅ **Old-file demotion**: 42-day-old file drops from raw score 0.43 to 0.163
4. ✅ **Candidate widening**: `limit × candidateMultiplier` (capped) ensures enough pre-decay candidates

### Test Results (all green)

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/temporal-decay.test.ts
 ✓ temporal-decay.test.ts (7 tests) 9ms
   ✓ matches exponential decay formula
   ✓ is 0.5 exactly at half-life
   ✓ does not decay evergreen memory files
   ✓ applies decay in hybrid merging before ranking
   ✓ handles future dates, zero age, and very old memories
   ✓ uses file mtime fallback for non-memory sources
   ✓ propagates config resolution errors instead of silently disabling decay
Tests: 7 passed (7)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
 ✓ qmd-manager.test.ts (128 tests) 188ms
Tests: 128 passed (128)
```

### Config Error Propagation Fix

Removed the `try/catch` fallback in `resolveTemporalDecaySearchConfig` that silently disabled decay when `resolveMemorySearchConfig` threw. Now the function lets canonical validation errors propagate, matching ClawSweeper's P2 recommendation. Regression test confirms null-config throws instead of returning defaults.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(auth): resolve custom provider secretref-managed apiKey from runtime snapshot
target.body:
## Summary

Custom providers with `apiKey: secretref-managed` (resolved via SecretRef) fail with 503 `auth_unavailable` after update to 2026.6.5. The auth resolution chain correctly blocks on the managed-secretref marker, but the fast availability check (`hasRuntimeAvailableProviderAuth`) and the final credential resolution path were using two different lookup strategies: resolution could fall back to the runtime config snapshot, while availability still rejected based on the source config alone.

Fixes #92097.

## Changes

- `src/agents/model-auth.ts`:
  - Extract `resolveManagedSecretRefCustomProviderApiKey` to look up a real API key in the active runtime config snapshot when the source config stores `secretref-managed` for a `models.providers` entry.
  - Use the shared helper in `hasRuntimeAvailableProviderAuth` so availability no longer rejects before resolution runs.
  - Use the shared helper in `resolveProviderSyntheticRuntimeAuth` for custom-provider managed SecretRefs, while preserving the existing plugin-side SecretRef runtime snapshot fallback.
- `src/agents/model-auth.test.ts`: Added regression tests for `hasRuntimeAvailableProviderAuth` covering both resolved and unresolved runtime snapshot states.
- `scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts`: Updated standalone reproduction to use real file-backed SecretRef preparation (`prepareSecretsRuntimeSnapshot`) plus a source config that only stores the managed marker, and asserts both availability and final resolution.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A custom provider with `secretref-managed` apiKey is now reported as available by the runtime hot-path check and resolves the actual key from the runtime config snapshot.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v22.19.0, local source checkout at `fix/92097-secretref-custom-provider-auth`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/model-auth.test.ts --run`
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== Reproduction for issue #92097 ===
Proving managed SecretRef custom-provider auth through actual file-based SecretRef preparation.

Runtime available auth: true
Resolved auth: {
  apiKey: 'resolved-from-file-secret',
  source: 'models.providers.cliproxyapi',
  mode: 'api-key'
}

PASS: Custom provider secretref-managed apiKey is available and correctly resolved from file-backed SecretRef runtime snapshot.
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `hasRuntimeAvailableProviderAuth` returns `true` and `resolveApiKeyForProvider` returns the real `apiKey` that was resolved from the on-disk secrets file via `prepareSecretsRuntimeSnapshot`, with correct `source` and `mode` fields.
- **What was not tested**: A live multi-hop gateway request through the full HTTP/WS stack; TLS-terminated gateway; other auth modes (OAuth, profile-based) for custom providers.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/model-auth.test.ts --run` — 61 passed (including new availability regression tests)
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts` — passed
- `npx oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts` — no further changes needed
- `pnpm tsgo:core` — passed
- `pnpm build` — passed
- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts` — PASS

target.title: fix(codex): return diagnostic snapshot on app inventory refresh failure
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes sub-problem #2 of #91352: native Codex `codexPlugins` app inventory refresh failures no longer propagate as unhandled rejections, so normal Codex turns can continue without plugin apps instead of failing the harness.

When `app/list` fails in `CodexAppInventoryCache.refreshUncoalesced`, the method now returns a best-effort `CodexAppInventorySnapshot` carrying a `lastError` diagnostic. Callers that already try/catch `refreshNow` (thread config and plugin activation) continue to work, and the cache no longer forces every consumer to handle rejection propagation.

## Changes

- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.ts`
  - Replace `throw error` in `refreshUncoalesced` with a diagnostic snapshot return.
  - If a previous successful snapshot exists, preserve its apps and attach `lastError`.
  - If no prior snapshot exists, return an empty snapshot with `expiresAtMs: 0` so `read()` still reports `missing` on the next lookup.

- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts`
  - Update existing test to assert resolve-with-diagnostic instead of rejection.
  - Add regression test for the first-refresh-failure case (empty cache, no prior entry).

## Verification

```bash
# Focused cache tests
pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts
# 8 passed

# Thread config tests that exercise refreshNow through buildCodexPluginThreadConfig
pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts
# 14 passed

# Plugin inventory tests
pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-inventory.test.ts
# 5 passed

# Lint
npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json   extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.ts   extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts
# clean
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Codex `app/list` refresh failures used to throw from `CodexAppInventoryCache.refreshNow`, which could fail the harness turn. After the patch, the cache returns a diagnostic snapshot so the turn continues without plugin apps.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout at commit `72b5ab5d76` on Node `$(node --version)`, running the actual `CodexAppInventoryCache` code (not a unit-test wrapper).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/91359-codex-app-list-failure-proof.mts
```

Script content: `scripts/repro/91359-codex-app-list-failure-proof.mts`

**Evidence after fix:**

```
=== PR #91359 Codex app/list failure proof ===

refreshNow returned (did not throw): true
snapshot.lastError: simulated app/list failure
snapshot.apps: []
snapshot.expiresAtMs: 0
cache.read state: missing
cache.read diagnostic: simulated app/list failure

PASS: failure handled gracefully.
```

**Observed result after fix:** When `refreshNow` is called with a request that throws, it returns a snapshot containing `lastError`, `apps: []`, and `expiresAtMs: 0` instead of rejecting. A subsequent `cache.read` reports `state: "missing"` and surfaces the diagnostic. This matches the behavior the harness relies on to continue a turn without plugin apps.

**What was not tested:** A live end-to-end Codex harness run against a real failing app server; this proof exercises the exact cache code path that the harness uses during turn setup.

Closes #91352 (sub-problem #2)

target.body:
## Summary

- Add opt-in terminal fallback metadata through agent-core, session tool definitions, subscribe handling, and the embedded runner.
- Surface safe terminal summaries for tool-loop and incomplete-turn exits, while keeping undeclared tool output neutral instead of leaking raw results.
- Declare structured fallbacks for `cron` and `web_fetch`, and keep Docker image builds from carrying stale `dist` output.
- Tighten delivery/side-effect evidence for channel replies, source-reply deliveries, context-engine pairing, compaction ownership, heartbeat lifecycle handling, and tool-plugin result preservation.

## Contract note

- `terminalSummary` and `terminalResultFallback` are intentionally public tool metadata for safe terminal presentation.
- Tools opt in explicitly; undeclared raw tool output is not surfaced as a fallback.
- Generic tool activity, accepted child-session spawns, messaging sends, cron additions, and replay-invalid aborts continue to suppress fallback model replay so side effects are not duplicated.
- `defineToolPlugin` preserves intentional helper-created tool results while continuing to JSON-wrap bare text-shaped objects with `content` and `details` fields.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/tool-plugin.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`

## Summary

- Fixes SecretRef-backed Gateway auth diagnostics where a reachable Gateway could look unhealthy in read-only CLI diagnostics when the client command could not resolve `gateway.auth.*` SecretRefs.
- Reuses the existing reachable-but-unauthenticated Gateway health diagnostic instead of weakening `callGateway` credential resolution.
- Keeps strict Gateway calls fail-closed on unresolved auth SecretRefs, while improving `health`, `gateway health`, `doctor`, `channels status`, and `browser doctor` output.
- Out of scope: changing Gateway auth precedence, resolving SecretRefs inside generic RPC callers, or broadening browser admin-scope access.

## Linked context

Closes #91815

Related: SecretRef-backed Google Chat/Gateway health diagnostics reported from 2026.6.5.

Requested by a maintainer in issue triage.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: SecretRef-backed `gateway.auth.password` was resolvable to the Gateway process but unavailable to client-side diagnostic commands, causing raw `GatewaySecretRefUnavailableError` or misleading unhealthy/unreachable output.
- Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux runner, provider `aws`, lease `cbx_3d12c04b5fd5`, run `run_3acc3db512f2`, OpenClaw source branch `fix/secretref-diagnostic-gateway-health-91815`.

- Evidence after fix: Crabbox run `run_3acc3db512f2` printed `SECRETREF_GATEWAY_HEALTH_PROOF_PASS` after checking all assertions.
- Observed result after fix: `gateway status --deep --json` succeeded; `health` and `gateway health` exited 1 with `Gateway: reachable` plus the credentials-required diagnostic; `channels status` exited 0 with `Gateway auth unavailable; showing config-only status.`; `browser doctor` exited 1 with a readable SecretRef message; `doctor --allow-exec` exited 0 and noted `Gateway credentials required`; strict `gateway call health --json` still exited 1 with `GatewaySecretRefUnavailableError`.

An earlier AWS attempt hit a remote install hang after `pnpm install` printed `Done`, so the passing proof used the hydrated checkout and direct `node dist/entry.js` after an explicit build.
